Shy Rhino is “it” in a game of hide-and-seek and finds her animal friends one by one — until clever Gecko hides where she’d never think to look.

💡 Did you know?

Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood.

Two of the hearts pump blood to the gills, and the third pumps it to the rest of the body. Their blood is blue because it carries oxygen using a copper-based molecule called hemocyanin, instead of the iron-based hemoglobin that makes our blood red.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A group of flamingos is called a 'flamboyance'.

Flamingos are pink because of what they eat — tiny algae and shrimp full of natural pink-orange pigments. A baby flamingo is actually born grey and slowly turns pink over its first couple of years.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Honeybees can recognize human faces.

Despite having a brain about the size of a sesame seed, honeybees can learn and remember individual human faces. They use a process called 'configural processing' — looking at how features like eyes, nose, and mouth are arranged together, rather than one feature at a time.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A snail can sleep for up to three years.

When the weather is too dry or too cold, some snails seal themselves inside their shell with a layer of dried mucus and enter a deep resting state (called estivation or hibernation). It usually lasts weeks or months, but in extreme conditions can stretch to about three years.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Sea otters hold hands while they sleep so they don't float apart.

Sea otters rest floating on their backs, and holding paws — called 'rafting' — keeps the group together so no one drifts away on the current. They also wrap themselves in seaweed to stay anchored in one spot.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Butterflies taste with their feet.

Butterflies have taste sensors on their feet, so just by standing on a leaf or flower they can tell whether it's a good place to lay eggs or a tasty spot to drink from.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Elephants are the only animals that can't jump.

Elephants are simply too heavy, and their legs are built to support enormous weight rather than to spring off the ground. They always keep at least one foot down — but they can still run quickly and are surprisingly good swimmers.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A giraffe's tongue can be about 50 cm long — and it's bluish-purple.

A giraffe uses its long, grippy tongue to pull leaves from between sharp thorns. The dark bluish-purple colour is thought to work like sunscreen, protecting the tongue from sunburn while it feeds out in the sun all day.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A group of owls is called a 'parliament'.

The name comes from old stories that pictured owls as wise creatures gathering like a council. Owls can also turn their heads about 270 degrees, which helps them look around because their eyes can't move in their sockets like ours.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A shrimp's heart is in its head.

A shrimp's heart sits just behind its head, in the front part of its body called the thorax — so it really does look like the heart is in the head. Many of its other organs are packed in there too.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Cows have best friends and can feel sad when they're apart.

Studies show cows form close bonds with particular herd-mates and get more stressed — their heart rate rises — when they're separated from a friend. They're social animals that feel calmer together.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Rats laugh when they are tickled.

When rats play or are gently tickled, they make high-pitched chirps that are too high for us to hear without special equipment. Scientists link these sounds to happy feelings — a little like human laughter.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Starfish don't have a brain.

A starfish has no brain and no blood. Instead it uses a simple network of nerves and moves seawater through its body to power hundreds of tiny tube feet. Many starfish can even regrow a lost arm.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Dolphins give each other names using special whistles.

As a baby, each dolphin invents its own unique 'signature whistle', and other dolphins can copy that whistle to call it — much like using a name.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A hummingbird's heart can beat more than 1,000 times a minute.

Hummingbirds beat their wings up to about 80 times every second, and their tiny hearts race to keep up. They're the only birds that can fly backwards, and they have to eat almost constantly to fuel all that energy.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

The blue whale is the biggest animal that has ever lived — bigger than any dinosaur.

A blue whale can grow to about 30 metres long and weigh as much as 30 elephants. Its heart is roughly the size of a small car, and a blue whale calf can gain around 90 kg a day just from its mother's milk.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Ants don't have lungs.

Ants are far too small for lungs. Instead, air flows in and out through tiny holes along the sides of their bodies, called spiracles, carrying oxygen straight to where it's needed.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Some penguins give a pebble to the penguin they like.

Gentoo penguins build their nests from stones, so a male will search for a smooth, just-right pebble and offer it to a female — a bit like giving a gift to ask, 'will you be my partner?'

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Your heart beats about 100,000 times every day.

That adds up to around 35 million beats a year, quietly pumping blood to every part of your body without ever taking a break. Over a whole lifetime it beats more than 2.5 billion times.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Babies are born with about 300 bones, but grown-ups have only 206.

As you grow, some soft, separate baby bones slowly fuse — join together — into single, stronger bones, for example in the skull and the spine. That's why adults end up with fewer bones than babies.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

You are a little bit taller in the morning than at night.

The soft cushions between the bones in your spine get gently squished as you stand and sit during the day. Lying down overnight lets them puff back up, so you're about 1 cm taller when you wake.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Your brain uses about a fifth of all the energy your body makes.

Even though the brain is only about 2% of your body weight, it uses roughly 20% of your energy. Thinking, sensing, and controlling your whole body takes a lot of power.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

The hardest part of your body is the enamel on your teeth.

Tooth enamel, the shiny outer layer, is even harder than bone. But unlike bone it can't repair itself once it's worn away — which is exactly why brushing to protect it matters so much.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

You blink around 15 to 20 times every minute.

Each blink spreads a thin layer of tears to clean and protect your eyes. It adds up to thousands of blinks a day, so your eyes are briefly shut for a small part of every hour without you noticing.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Your nose can remember thousands of different smells.

Smell is closely linked to memory in the brain, which is why a single scent can suddenly remind you of a place, a food, or a person from long ago.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

If you lined up all your blood vessels, they could wrap around the Earth more than twice.

Your arteries, veins, and tiny capillaries together stretch roughly 100,000 km. Most of them are microscopic capillaries — thinner than a hair — delivering oxygen to every cell in your body.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Your small intestine is about 6 metres long — longer than a car!

It's coiled up neatly inside your belly. Its job is to soak up the goodness from your food, and its walls are folded and fuzzy to give a huge surface area for absorbing nutrients.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Your fingerprints are all your own — no one else in the world has the same ones.

Fingerprints form before you're even born and stay the same your whole life. Even identical twins, who share the same DNA, have completely different fingerprints.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Carrots used to be purple before they were orange.

The earliest carrots were purple, white, or yellow. Orange carrots became popular only a few hundred years ago in the Netherlands, and now they're the ones we see most often.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Chocolate grows on trees — it comes from the cacao fruit.

Chocolate starts as seeds (cocoa beans) inside the pods of the cacao tree. The beans are fermented, dried, roasted, and ground, then mixed with sugar and milk to become the chocolate we eat.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Popcorn pops because the water inside each kernel turns to steam.

Every kernel holds a little water inside a hard shell. When it heats up, the water becomes steam and builds pressure until — pop! — the kernel bursts and turns inside out into fluffy popcorn.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Apples float in water because they are about a quarter air.

Around 25% of an apple is made up of tiny air pockets, which makes it light enough to float. That's exactly why the game of 'apple bobbing' works.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Peanuts aren't really nuts — they're in the same family as beans.

Peanuts are legumes, like peas and beans, and they grow underground rather than on trees. True tree nuts include almonds, walnuts, and cashews.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Cucumbers are about 96% water.

That's why cucumbers taste so cool and refreshing. Being almost all water, they're very light and a nice way to help you stay hydrated on a hot day.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A pineapple can take almost two years to grow.

A pineapple plant grows slowly and makes just one pineapple at a time, taking roughly 18 to 24 months from planting to a ripe, juicy fruit.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A strawberry can have around 200 tiny seeds on the outside.

Those little specks on a strawberry's skin are its seeds — and each one could grow into a whole new plant. Carrying seeds on the outside is one reason a strawberry isn't a true berry.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Bananas are berries, but strawberries are not.

To scientists, a 'berry' is a soft fruit that grows from a single flower with its seeds inside — which fits bananas (and grapes and tomatoes). A strawberry grows differently and carries its seeds on the outside, so it isn't a true berry.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Honey never goes bad — honey found in ancient tombs is still good to eat.

Honey has very little water and is slightly acidic, so bacteria and mould can't grow in it. Archaeologists have even found pots of 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that were still edible.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Lightning is about five times hotter than the surface of the Sun.

A lightning bolt can heat the air around it to roughly 30,000°C for a split second. That sudden heat makes the air expand with a bang — the thunder we hear.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Rainbows are actually full circles — we usually just see the top half.

A rainbow is really a complete ring of light, but the ground hides the bottom part. From high up in an aeroplane, you can sometimes see a rainbow as a full circle.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Sunflowers turn to follow the sun across the sky.

Young sunflowers slowly turn their heads from east to west during the day to catch the most sunlight, then swing back at night. This is called heliotropism. Once fully grown, they usually settle facing east.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Some trees can live for thousands of years.

Certain bristlecone pine trees are more than 4,000 years old — already ancient back when the pyramids were young. They survive by growing very slowly in harsh, rocky mountain places.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

The Sahara Desert sometimes gets cold enough to snow.

Desert air is very dry and holds little heat, so nights can turn freezing. Every so often the town of Ain Sefra, on the edge of the Sahara, has actually seen real snowfall.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Water is the only thing on Earth found naturally as a solid, a liquid, and a gas.

You can spot all three in everyday nature: ice (solid), rain or the sea (liquid), and invisible water vapour or clouds (gas). Most other substances need extreme temperatures to switch states like that.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

No two snowflakes are exactly the same.

Each snowflake grows its own six-sided pattern depending on the exact temperature and moisture it drifts through on its way down. With so many tiny differences, finding two identical ones is practically impossible.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A bolt of lightning can strike the same spot many times.

The old saying 'lightning never strikes twice' isn't true. Tall pointy objects like the Empire State Building get struck dozens of times a year, because their height and shape attract lightning.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

The Amazon rainforest is home to more than 3 million kinds of plants and animals.

The Amazon is the most life-packed place on land, and new species are still being discovered there all the time. A single tree can be home to more kinds of insects than some whole countries.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Tiny plants in the ocean make a large part of the oxygen we breathe.

Microscopic ocean plants called phytoplankton produce roughly half of the world's oxygen through photosynthesis — so the sea, not just forests, helps power every breath you take.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A 'googol' is a 1 followed by one hundred zeros.

That's a mind-bogglingly big number — even bigger than the number of atoms we think are in the whole visible universe. The company Google was named after it, with a little spelling twist.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Zero is the only number that can't be written with Roman numerals.

Romans wrote numbers using letters like I, V, X, and M, but they had no symbol for zero at all. The idea of zero as its own number reached Europe later, from Indian and Arabic mathematics.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

If you could fold a piece of paper 42 times, it would reach all the way to the Moon.

Each fold doubles the thickness, and doubling grows unbelievably fast. After 42 folds the paper would be about 400,000 km thick. In real life, though, paper can only be folded a handful of times.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

The word 'four' is spelled with four letters — the only number in English like that.

F-O-U-R has exactly four letters, matching its own value. No other English number word matches its number, which makes 'four' a fun one-of-a-kind.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

In a room of just 23 people, it's more likely than not that two share a birthday.

It feels surprising, but you're comparing every possible pair of people, not just yourself against the others. With 23 people there are 253 possible pairs, which tips it over 50%. This is called the 'birthday paradox'.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Any number multiplied by zero is always zero.

Multiplying is like adding a number that many times. Multiplying by zero means adding it no times at all — so you're left with nothing, which is zero.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A 'dozen' means 12, and a 'baker's dozen' means 13.

Long ago, bakers added an extra loaf to every dozen so they wouldn't get in trouble for accidentally selling bread that was a bit too small or light. Thirteen-for-twelve became known as a baker's dozen.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A 'googolplex' is an even bigger number — a 1 followed by a googol of zeros.

A googolplex is so enormous that you could never write out all its zeros. There isn't enough room in the entire universe to print them, even in the tiniest possible writing.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

The Sun is so big that about 1.3 million Earths could fit inside it.

The Sun holds about 99.8% of all the mass in our Solar System. It's a giant ball of hot glowing gas, and its gravity is what keeps every planet circling around it.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

A day on Venus is longer than a whole year on Venus.

Venus spins very slowly — one full turn (a day) takes about 243 Earth days, while one trip around the Sun (a year) takes about 225 Earth days. So a single day on Venus lasts longer than its year.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Space is completely silent because there is no air to carry sound.

Sound travels as vibrations through air, water, or solids. Space is almost a perfect vacuum with hardly any particles, so those vibrations have nothing to travel through — and no sound can be heard.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Footprints left on the Moon can stay for millions of years.

The Moon has no wind and no rain to wear things away, so the footprints the Apollo astronauts left in the 1960s and 70s are still there — and could last for millions of years.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Jupiter is the largest planet in our Solar System.

Jupiter is so huge that all the other planets could fit inside it together. Its famous Great Red Spot is a swirling storm bigger than the whole Earth that has been going for hundreds of years.

More fun facts for kids on KidSafe Play: https://genai.io.vn/facts

💡 Did you know?

Saturn is so light for its size that it would float in water.

Saturn is made mostly of gas, so it's less dense than water — if you had a bathtub big enough, Saturn would float in it. It's also famous for its bright rings, made of countless pieces of ice and rock.

💡 Did you know?

It takes about 8 minutes for sunlight to travel from the Sun to Earth.

Light is the fastest thing in the universe, yet the Sun is so far away (about 150 million km) that its light still takes roughly 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach us. We always see the Sun as it looked a few minutes ago.

💡 Did you know?

Mars is home to the tallest volcano in the Solar System, called Olympus Mons.

Olympus Mons is about three times taller than Mount Everest — roughly 22 km high — and nearly as wide as the whole country of Italy. Mars looks red because its soil is full of rusty iron.

💡 Did you know?

There are more stars in the sky than grains of sand on all the world's beaches.

Astronomers estimate there are hundreds of billions of galaxies, each holding billions of stars. Added up, the number of stars is far greater than every grain of sand on every beach on Earth.

💡 Did you know?

One year on Neptune lasts about 165 Earth years.

Neptune is so far from the Sun that it takes about 165 Earth years to travel around it just once. Since it was discovered in 1846, it has completed only one full orbit (finished in 2011).

💡 Did you know?

The Moon moves a tiny bit farther from Earth every year.

The Moon drifts away from us by about 3.8 cm each year — roughly the speed your fingernails grow. Very slowly, this is also making Earth's days a little bit longer over millions of years.

💡 Did you know?

A single teaspoon of a neutron star would weigh billions of tons.

A neutron star is what's left after a giant star collapses. Its material is squeezed together so tightly that just one teaspoonful would weigh about as much as a whole mountain.
